# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 4

This is turn 4. Turns 1 through 3 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 4.

# Turn 4 — the \(\sigma_2\)-transvection candidate

Turn 3 isolated the exact conflict: \(R_0\) preserves every tested tame-open Demushkin pairing but misses \(GL_2(3)\), while the linear defect \(d_0d_1^{-1}\) repairs \(GL_2(3)\) and destroys those pairings. 

The surviving correction is neither linear nor invisible at quadratic order. It is a commutator with the **pro-\(2\) projection of Frobenius**.

## Candidate \(A_2\)

Using the supplied residue \(\omega_2=40491355905\),  submit:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

Thus
\[
A_2=R_0[d_0,x_0^{\sigma_2}],
\qquad
[d_0,z]=d_0^{-1}z^{-1}d_0z,
\qquad
\sigma_2=\sigma^{\omega_2}.
\]

The implicit tame relation remains \(\tau^\sigma=\tau^2\).

## Why a third-order correction cannot work

Suppose a correction has zero linear and quadratic Magnus terms in every tame kernel. It then lies in the third mod-\(2\) dimension subgroup \(D_3\). Under a map to \(GL_2(3)\), the wild kernel lands in
\[
O_2(GL_2(3))\cong Q_8.
\]
But
\[
D_3(Q_8)=Q_8^4[Q_8,Q_8]^2[Q_8,Q_8,Q_8]=1.
\]
Such a correction therefore evaluates trivially and cannot repair the zero \(GL_2(3)\)-count of \(R_0\).

So the needed word must alter the quadratic form, but only by an invertible—indeed unipotent—change. The commutator \(c_0\) does exactly that.

## Uniform tame-open calculation

Let
\[
H(e,n)=\langle s,t\mid s^n=t^e=1,\ t^s=t^2\rangle ,
\]
where \(e\) is odd and \(\operatorname{ord}_e(2)\mid n\). These are all finite tame quotients. Let \(U(e,n)\) be the kernel of the corresponding map from the candidate group.

Reidemeister–Schreier gives \(3en+1\) generators and \(2en\) relators. The mod-\(2\) linear relation matrix has rank \(2en-1\), so
\[
\dim H^1(U(e,n),\mathbf F_2)=en+2,\qquad
\dim H^2(U(e,n),\mathbf F_2)=1,
\]
as required for a degree-\(en\) extension of \(\mathbf Q_2\).

Write
\[
n=qr,\qquad q=2^{v_2(n)},\qquad r\ \text{odd},
\]
and let \(s_2=s^{\omega_2}\), which has order \(q\). If \(B_0\) denotes the perfect cup-product matrix produced by \(R_0\), the Fox calculation for \(A_2\) gives, after linear elimination,
\[
B_{A_2}=B_0(I+N).
\]

Up to a change of basis,
\[
N=
\begin{cases}
1+s_2^2 &\text{on }I(\mathbf F_2[C_e])\otimes\mathbf F_2[C_n],\\
0&\text{on a complementary subspace of dimension }n+2.
\end{cases}
\]

Since \(s_2^2\) has \(2\)-power order,
\[
(1+s_2^2)^{q/2}=1+s_2^q=0
\]
in characteristic \(2\). Hence \(N\) is nilpotent and \(I+N\) is invertible. Therefore \(B_{A_2}\) is perfect for every \(e,n\).

More precisely, for \(q\ge4\),
\[
\operatorname{rank}(N^j)
=
2(e-1)r\max\!\left(\frac q2-j,0\right).
\]
Equivalently, the nonzero part of \(N\) consists of \(2(e-1)r\) nilpotent Jordan blocks of size \(q/2\). For \(q\le2\), \(N=0\).

Thus every finite tame-open kernel of \(A_2\) has a maximal pro-\(2\) quotient with the required Demushkin dimensions and nondegenerate cup product.

This also explains why a fixed exponent such as \(\sigma^3\) looked promising but was not uniform:

| tame inertia \(e\) | required rank | correction using \(\sigma^3\) | correction using \(\sigma_2\) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| \(7\) | \(23\) | \(23\) | \(23\) |
| \(19\) | \(344\) | \(236\) | \(344\) |
| \(27\) | \(488\) | \(332\) | \(488\) |
| \(29\) | \(814\) | — | \(814\) |

The failures occur when a fixed integer fails to annihilate enough of the odd-order component of Frobenius. The idempotent \(\omega_2\) removes that entire component uniformly.

## Structural classes retained

Because \(c_0\) is a commutator inside the wild core:

- When the target is a \(2\)-group, \(\tau=1\), hence \(u_0=x_0\), \(d_0=1\), and \(c_0=1\). The relation is exactly
  \[
  x_0^2x_1^3x_1^\sigma=1.
  \]
- In an abelian target, \(c_0=1\), and the abelianization remains the correct one.
- If \(O_2(G)=1\), the correction is trivial.
- If \(O_2(G)\) is elementary abelian, the correction is trivial. Consequently all of the Turn-2 elementary-core theorem and all earlier elementary-core finite counts remain valid unchanged.

## New exact finite-quotient checks

Full enumeration gives:

| Target \(G\) | \(|\mathrm{Sur}(A_2,G)|\) | \(|\mathrm{Aut}(G)|\) | quotient | ground truth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| \(SL_2(3)\) | \(0\) | \(24\) | \(0\) | \(0\) |
| \(GL_2(3)\) | \(384\) | \(48\) | \(8\) | \(8\) |
| \(GL_2(3)\times C_2\) | \(2304\) | \(192\) | \(12\) | \(12\) |
| \(GL_2(3)\times C_3\) | \(768\) | \(96\) | \(8\) | \(8\) |

For \(GL_2(3)\times C_2\), the ground-truth value \(12\) can be recovered without a database. There are eight \(GL_2(3)\)-extensions and seven quadratic extensions. Each \(GL_2(3)\)-extension contains its unique quadratic subfield, leaving six disjoint choices, hence \(48\) pairs. Each resulting \(GL_2(3)\times C_2\)-extension is represented by four such pairs, giving \(48/4=12\).

For \(GL_2(3)\times C_3\), the unique cyclic cubic extension of \(\mathbf Q_2\) is unramified and is disjoint from every \(GL_2(3)\)-extension, giving eight composita.

The reproducible exact enumeration and RS/Magnus calculation are here:

[Turn 4 \(\sigma_2\)-candidate certificate](sandbox:/mnt/data/turn4_sigma2_candidate_certificate.py)

**ESTABLISHED**: Found the explicit candidate \(A_2=R_0[d_0,x_0^{\sigma_2}]\). Proved that a genuinely third-order correction cannot repair the \(GL_2(3)\) obstruction. Derived the uniform tame-kernel pairing update \(B_{A_2}=B_0(I+N)\), with \(N\) nilpotent because it acts as \(1+s_2^2\) on the nontrivial inertia-character component. Consequently every finite tame-open kernel tested—and the resulting all-parameter normal form—has the required Demushkin pairing. Exact enumeration gives the correct counts for \(GL_2(3)\), \(SL_2(3)\), \(GL_2(3)\times C_2\), and \(GL_2(3)\times C_3\), while preserving all previously solved elementary-core targets.

**GAP**: \(A_2\) has not been run against the official 5402-group verifier. Its obstruction distribution remains untested on general non-elementary \(2\)-cores not generated from the \(Q_8\) layer, especially other central Frattini covers of \(S_4\)-type and higher quaternionic/dihedral layers.

**NEXT**: Treat \(A_2\) as the new baseline and perform a systematic central-Frattini-cover sweep, grouped by extension class rather than by isolated target: compute its obstruction function on every central \(C_2\)-cover of the already-closed elementary-core quotients, then advance layer-by-layer through \(\Phi(O_2(G))\).
